subject: Mathematics
q: The number of values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> for which the system of linear equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> has no solution is
question: The number of values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> for which the system of linear equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> has no solution is
solution:
For no solution

k+2k=10k+3=kk-1

⇒k+2k+3=10k

⇒k2-5k+6=0⇒k=2, 3

k≠2 for k=2 both lines are identical.

So, only k=3 is possible.

So the number of values of k is 1.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the system of equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>β</mi></math>. Has infinitely many solutions, then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If the system of equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>β</mi></math>. Has infinitely many solutions, then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
We know that, for infinitely many solutions,

Δ=0=Δx=Δy=Δz

So, Δ=α11123135=0

⇒α10-9-15-3+13-2=0

⇒α-2+1=0

⇒α=1

Now checking for Δx=511423β35=0

⇒510-9-120-3β+112-2β

⇒5-20+3β+12-2β

⇒-3+β=0

⇒β=3

Hence α,β=1,3

subject: Mathematics
q:
The set of all values of λ for which the system of linear equations
x-2y-2z=λx
x+2y+z=λy
-x-y=λz
has a non-trivial solution :
question:
The set of all values of λ for which the system of linear equations
x-2y-2z=λx
x+2y+z=λy
-x-y=λz
has a non-trivial solution :
solution:
For non-trivial solution, ∆=0.

⇒1-λ-2-212-λ1-1-1-λ=0

⇒1-λ-2λ+λ2+1+1-λ+1-1 -1+2-λ=0

⇒λ2-3λ2+3λ-1=0

⇒λ-13=0

⇒λ=1

Singleton set

subject: Mathematics
q:
If S is the set of distinct values of b for which the following system of linear equations

x+y+z=1

x+ay+z=1

ax+by+z=0

has no solution, then S  is:
question:
If S is the set of distinct values of b for which the following system of linear equations

x+y+z=1

x+ay+z=1

ax+by+z=0

has no solution, then S  is:
options: A finite set containing two or more elements
solution:
For no solution D=1111a1ab1=0

Expanding the determinant along the first row, we get D=-a-12=0

⇒   a=1

For a=1, first two planes are coincident.

Now, we have x+y+z=1 & ax+by+z=0

For no solution, these two planes are parallel.

⇒1a=1b=11

⇒a=1 & b=1.

∴   b=1 ⇒ it is a singleton set

subject: Mathematics
q: If a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>α</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>β</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>γ</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mfenced><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>10</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>9</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left left left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> lies on the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mi>γ</mi></math> is equal to
question: If a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>α</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>β</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>γ</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mfenced><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>10</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>9</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>8</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mtable columnalign="left left left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> lies on the plane <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>β</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mi>γ</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

αβγ2108938848=000

⇒2α+9β+8γ10α+3β+4γ8α+8β+8γ=000

Now on comparing both side we get,

2α+2β+8γ=0        …1

10α+3β+4γ=0         …2

8α+8β+8γ=0         …3

Now from 1 and 3 by cross multiplication we get,

α1=β6=γ-7=k⇒α=k, β=6k, γ=-7k

Point Pα,β,γ lie on the plane 2x+4y+3z=5⇒2α+4β+3γ=5

⇒2k+24k-21k=5

⇒k=1

Hence, 6α+9β+7γ=6k+54k-49k=11k=11.

subject: Mathematics
q: For real numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo></math> consider the following system of linear equations: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the system has infinite solutions, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to ______.
question: For real numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo></math> consider the following system of linear equations: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the system has infinite solutions, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
Given system of linear equations are x+y-z=2,x+2y+αz=1,2x-y+z=β

Using Cramer Rule, we can say for infinite solutions

Δ=Δ1=Δ2=Δ3=0

Here, Δ=11-112α2-11=0

Apply R1↔R1+R3

⇒30012α2-11=0

⇒32+α=0

⇒α=-2

And Δ2=12-111-22β1=0

⇒11+2β-21+4-β-2=0

⇒β-7=0

⇒β=7

∴ α+β=5

subject: Mathematics
q: If the system of equations <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a non-trivial solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mi>z</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>z</mi><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
question: If the system of equations <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has a non-trivial solution <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mi>z</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>z</mi><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
The system of equations a1x+b1y+c1z=0, a2x+b2y+c2z=0 and a3x+b3y+c3z=0 has a non-trivial solution, if 

a1b1c1a2b2c2a3b3c3=0.

Hence, for the given lines 2x+3y-z=0, x+ky-2z=0 and 2x-y+z=0, we have

23-11k-22-11=0

⇒2k-2-31+4-1-1-2k=0

⇒2k-4-15+1+2k=0

⇒k=92

Thus, the lines are 

2x+3y-z=0   ...1

x+92y-2z=0   ...2

2x-y+z=0    ...3

1-3⇒4y-2z=0

2y=z     ...4

yz=12     ...5

put z from eq 4 into 1

2x+3y-2y=0

2x+y=0

xy=-12     ...6

From eq 4 and 6

zx=-4

Hence, xy+yz+zx+k=-12+12-4+92=12.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let p and p+2 be prime numbers and let
Δ=p!p+1!p+2!p+1!p+2!p+3!p+2!p+3!p+4!
Then the sum of the maximum values of α and β, such that pα and p+2β divide Δ, is _______.
question:
Let p and p+2 be prime numbers and let
Δ=p!p+1!p+2!p+1!p+2!p+3!p+2!p+3!p+4!
Then the sum of the maximum values of α and β, such that pα and p+2β divide Δ, is _______.
solution:
Given,

Δ=P!P+1!P+2!P+1!P+2!P+3!P+2!P+3!P+4!

Now using the factorial concept and taking common terms we get,

⇒Δ=P!P+1!P+2!111P+1P+2P+3P+2P+1P+3P+2P+4P+3

Now on solving determinant we get,  111P+1P+2P+3P+2P+1P+3P+2P+4P+3

Using operation C1→C1-C2 & C2→C2-C3

001-1-1P+3-2P-4-2P-6P+4P+3

=2P+6-2P+4=2

Now putting the value of determinant we get,

⇒Δ=2P!P+1!P+2!

⇒Δ=2P3P-1!P+1P-1!P+2P+1P-1!

Which is divisible by Pα and P+2β

So, α=3,β=1

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>3</mn></math> matrix with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>det</mo><mfenced><mi>A</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> denote the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>i</mi><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> row of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. If a matrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is obtained by performing the operation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>det</mo><mfenced><mi>B</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>3</mn></math> matrix with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>det</mo><mfenced><mi>A</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></math> denote the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>i</mi><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> row of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. If a matrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is obtained by performing the operation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>det</mo><mfenced><mi>B</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to :
solution:
Let A=a11a12a13a21a22a23a31a32a33⇒2A=2a112a122a132a212a222a232a312a322a33

R2→2R2+5R3

B=2a112a122a134a21+10a314a22+10a324a23+10a332a312a322a33

B=2a112a122a134a21+10a314a22+10a324a23+10a332a312a322a33

B=2a112a122a134a214a224a232a312a322a33+2a112a122a1310a3110a3210a332a312a322a33

B=2×4×2 a11a12a13a21a22a23a31a32a33+2×10×2 a11a12a13a31a32a33a31a32a33

B=16 a11a12a13a21a22a23a31a32a33 +0 

B=16A

Value of the second determinant is zero. Since Row 2 and Row 3 are identical.

|B|=16×4=64∵|A|=4

subject: Mathematics
q: If the minimum and the maximum values of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>θ</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>12</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>10</mn></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>respectively, then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to :
question: If the minimum and the maximum values of the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>θ</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>12</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>10</mn></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>respectively, then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to :
solution:
C2→C2-C1

fθ=-sin2θ-11-cos2θ-1112-2-2=4cos2θ−sin2θ=4cos2θ

Since cos2θ=cos2θ-sin2θ

Again, π4≤θ≤π2⇒2π4≤2θ≤2π2

⇒π2≤θ≤π∴ θ∈π4,π2⇒2θ∈π2,π i.e., Second Quadrant.

⇒-1≤cos2θ≤0

fθmax=M=0

fθmin =m=-4

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of linear equations

2x+3y-z=-2

x+y+z=4

x-y+λz=4λ-4 where λ∈ℝ,

has no solution, then
question:
If the system of linear equations

2x+3y-z=-2

x+y+z=4

x-y+λz=4λ-4 where λ∈ℝ,

has no solution, then
solution:
If we have three equations in three variables, 

a1x+b1y+c1z=d1a2x+b2y+c2z=d2a3x+b3y+c3z=d3

then ∆=a1b1c1a2b2c2a3b3c3,∆1=d1b1c1d2b2c2d3b3c3,∆2=a1d1c1a2d2c2a3d3c3,∆3=a1b1d1a2b2d2a3b3d3.

If the set of equations do not have a solution then ∆=0 and at least one among ∆1,∆2,∆3 is not equal to zero.

∆=23-11111-1λ=0

⇒λ=7⇒λ=±7     …1

Case 1: λ=7

The system of equations are 

2x+3y-z=-2x+y+z=4x-y+7z=24

a2x+3y-z+2+bx+y+z-4=x-y+7z-24

Comparing the coefficients of x and y we get, 

2a+b=13a+b=-1⇒a=-2, b=5

For these values of a and b, coefficients of z and the constant values are matching. So the set of equations will have infinitely many solutions.

Case 2: λ=-7

The system of equations are 

2x+3y-z=-2x+y+z=4x-y-7z=-32

a2x+3y-z+2+bx+y+z-4=x-y-7z+32

Comparing the coefficients of x and y we get, 

2a+b=13a+b=-1⇒a=-2, b=5

For these values of a and b, coefficients of z and the constant values are not matching. So the set of equations will have no solutions.

D=11124-132λ=0⇒λ=92

D3=11224632μ=0⇒μ=5

Now check each option

subject: Mathematics
q:
The sum of all possible values of $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$, for which the system of equations :
$x\cos 3\theta - 8y - 12z = 0$
$x\cos 2\theta + 3y + 3z = 0$
$x + y + 3z = 0$
has a non-trivial solution, is equal to :
question:
The sum of all possible values of $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$, for which the system of equations :
$x\cos 3\theta - 8y - 12z = 0$
$x\cos 2\theta + 3y + 3z = 0$
$x + y + 3z = 0$
has a non-trivial solution, is equal to :
solution:
For the given system of homogeneous linear equations to have a non-trivial solution, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must be zero.

$\begin{vmatrix} \cos 3\theta & -8 & -12 \\ \cos 2\theta & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 3 \end{vmatrix} = 0$

Expanding the determinant along the first row:

$\cos 3\theta (9 - 3) - (-8)(3\cos 2\theta - 3) + (-12)(\cos 2\theta - 3) = 0$

$6\cos 3\theta + 24\cos 2\theta - 24 - 12\cos 2\theta + 36 = 0$

$6\cos 3\theta + 12\cos 2\theta + 12 = 0$

Dividing by $6$, we get:

$\cos 3\theta + 2\cos 2\theta + 2 = 0$

Using the multiple angle formulas $\cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3 \theta - 3\cos \theta$ and $\cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2 \theta - 1$, we substitute these into the equation:

$(4\cos^3 \theta - 3\cos \theta) + 2(2\cos^2 \theta - 1) + 2 = 0$

$4\cos^3 \theta + 4\cos^2 \theta - 3\cos \theta = 0$

$\cos \theta (4\cos^2 \theta + 4\cos \theta - 3) = 0$

$\cos \theta (2\cos \theta - 1)(2\cos \theta + 3) = 0$

This gives the possible values for $\cos \theta$:

$\cos \theta = 0 \implies \theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$

$\cos \theta = \dfrac{1}{2} \implies \theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}, \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

$\cos \theta = -\dfrac{3}{2}$ (Not possible since $\cos \theta \in [-1, 1]$)

The possible values of $\theta \in [0, 2\pi]$ are $\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}, \dfrac{\pi}{3}, \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$.

Sum of all possible values of $\theta$:

$\sum \theta = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} + \dfrac{3\pi}{2}\right) + \left(\dfrac{\pi}{3} + \dfrac{5\pi}{3}\right) = 2\pi + 2\pi = 4\pi$

Answer: $4\pi$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $a, b, c$, are non zero complex numbers satisfying $a^2+b^2+c^2=0$ and
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}b^2+c^2 & a b & a c \\ a b & c^2+a^2 & b c \\ a c & b c & a^2+b^2\end{array}\right|=k a^2 b^2 c^2$, then $k$ is equal to
question:
If $a, b, c$, are non zero complex numbers satisfying $a^2+b^2+c^2=0$ and
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}b^2+c^2 & a b & a c \\ a b & c^2+a^2 & b c \\ a c & b c & a^2+b^2\end{array}\right|=k a^2 b^2 c^2$, then $k$ is equal to
solution:
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}b^2+c^2 & a b & a c \\ a b & c^2+a^2 & b c \\ a c & b c & a^2+b^2\end{array}\right|$
Multiply $C_1$ by $a, C_2$ by b and $C_3$ by $\mathrm{c}$ and hence divide by $a b c$.

$$
=\frac{1}{a b c}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a\left(b^2+c^2\right. & \left.a b^2\right) & a c^2 \\
a^2 b & b\left(c^2+a^2\right. & \left.b c^2\right) \\
a^2 c & b^2 c & c\left(a^2+b^2\right.
\end{array}\right|
$$
Take out $a, b, c$ common from $R_1, R_2$ and $R_3$ respectively.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \Delta=\frac{a b c}{a b c}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
b^2+c^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\
a^2 & c^2+a^2 & c^2 \\
a^2 & b^2 & a^2+b^2
\end{array}\right| \\
& \text { Apply } C_1 \rightarrow C_1-C_2-C_3 \\
& \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & b^2 & c^2 \\
-2 c^2 & c^2+a^2 & c^2 \\
-2 b^2 & b^2 & a^2+b^2
\end{array}\right| \\
& =-2\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & b^2 & c^2 \\
c^2 & c^2+a^2 & c^2 \\
b^2 & b^2 & a^2+b^2
\end{array}\right| \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
Apply $C_2-C_1$ and $C_3-C_1$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =-2\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & b^2 & c^2 \\
c^2 & a^2 & 0 \\
b^2 & 0 & a^2
\end{array}\right| \\
& =-2\left[-b^2\left(c^2 a^2\right)+c^2\left(-a^2 b^2\right)\right] \\
& =2 a^2 b^2 c^2+2 a^2 b^2 c^2=4 a^2 b^2 c^2 \\
& \text { But } \Delta=k a^2 b^2 c^2 \therefore k=4
\end{aligned}
$$

q: Suppose the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> are the solutions of the system of linear equations, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi></math> when the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> on the right side is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, then the determinant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to
question: Suppose the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> are the solutions of the system of linear equations, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi></math> when the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> on the right side is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math>, then the determinant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Let A=α1α2α3β1β2β3γ1γ2γ3. Now satisfying x1, x2, x3 in Ax=b
Ax1=b1⇒α1α2α3β1β2β3γ1γ2γ3111=100

⇒α1+α2+α3=1β1+β2+β3=0γ1+γ2+γ3=0… i

Now 

Ax2=b2⇒α1α2α3β1β2β3γ1γ2γ3021=020

⇒2α2+α3=02β2+β3=22γ2+γ3=0… ii

And satisfying x3 gives,

α3=0, β3=0 and γ3=2

Now using i and ii

α2=0, β2=1, γ2=-1 and α1=1, β1=-1, γ1=-1
Thus, A=100-110-1-12⇒A=2

question: If <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mtable><mtr><mtd><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Put x=1 on both the sides

22-1430611=a-12

⇒23-0-24-0-14-18=a-12

⇒6-8+14=a-12

⇒12=a-12

⇒24=a.

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then the minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>det</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>A</mi></mfenced></mrow><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd><mtd><mi>b</mi></mtd><mtd><mn>2</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then the minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>det</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>A</mi></mfenced></mrow><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is:
solution:
A=2b1bb2+1b1b2

=22b2+2-b2-b2b-b+1b2-b2-1

=2b2+2-b2-1

=2b2+4-b2-1

=b2+3

Ab=b+3b=b-3b2+23

Hence, the minimum value of Ab is 23.  ∵b-3b2≥0

αx+1+βy+z=3
αx+βy+2z=2 has a unique solution, is :
question:

1+αx+βy+z=2
αx+1+βy+z=3
αx+βy+2z=2 has a unique solution, is :
solution:
For unique solution 1+αβ1α1+β1αβ2≠0

Applying C1→C1+C2+C3, we get

α+β+2β1α+β+21+β1α+β+2β2≠0

⇒α+β+21β111+β11β2≠0

⇒α+β+2≠0

Clearly, point 2, 4 satisfying the given condition.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Two fair dice are thrown. The numbers on them are taken as λ and μ, and a system of linear equations
x+y+z=5

x+2y+3z=μ

x+3y+λz=1
is constructed. If p is the probability that the system has a unique solution and q is the probability that the system has no solution, then:
question:
Two fair dice are thrown. The numbers on them are taken as λ and μ, and a system of linear equations
x+y+z=5

x+2y+3z=μ

x+3y+λz=1
is constructed. If p is the probability that the system has a unique solution and q is the probability that the system has no solution, then:
solution:
11112313λ=λ-5

Δ1=11523μ3λ1=10λ+3μ-λμ-44

Δ2=11513μ1λ1=5λ+μ-λμ-13

Δ3=11512μ131=-2μ+6

For unique solution
λ≠5,μ∈{1,2,3,4,5,6}

⇒p=56×1=56
For No Solution
λ=5, μ≠3

⇒  q=16×56=536

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of linear equations

2x+y-z=3

x-y-z=α

3x+3y+βz=3

has infinitely many solutions, then α+β-αβ is equal to __________.
question:
If the system of linear equations

2x+y-z=3

x-y-z=α

3x+3y+βz=3

has infinitely many solutions, then α+β-αβ is equal to __________.
solution:
Given,

2x+y-z=3

x-y-z=α

3x+3y+βz=3

Let A= 21-11-1-133β  & B=3α3

For Equation to have infinitely many solutions A=0 & Adj. A.B=0

A=21-11-1-133β=0

⇒-3β-3=0

⇒β=-1

Now,

Minor of A=2β+3β+36β+32β+33-2-1-3

Cofactor of A=-2β+3-β+3-6-β+3-2β+3-3213

Adjoint of A=-2β+3-β+32-β+3-2β+31-6-33

Now, adj. A.B=0

⇒-2β+3-β+32-β+3-2β+31-6-333α3=0

-6β-9-αβ-3α+6-3β-9-2αβ-3α+3-18-3α+9=0

⇒-18-3α+9=0

⇒α=-3

Therefore,α+β-αβ=-3-1-3=-7=7

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the following system of equations:

question:
Consider the following system of equations: 

x+2y-3z=a

2x+6y-11z=b

x-2y+7z=c

where a,b and c are real constants. Then the system of equations :
options: has no solution for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math>
has infinite number of solutions when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math>
has a unique solution for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math>
solution:
D=12-326-111-27

=20-2(25)-3(-10)

=20-50+30=0

D1=a2-3b6-11c-27

=20a-2(7b+11c)-3(-2b-6c)

=20a-14b-22c+6b+18c

=20a-8b-4c

=4(5a-2b-c)

D2=1a-32b-111c7

=7b+11c-a(25)-3(2c-b)

=7b+11c-25a-6c+3b

=-25a+10b+5c

=-5(5a-2b-c)

D3=12a26b1-2c

=6c+2b-2(2c-b)-10a

=-10a+4b+2c

=-2(5a-2b-c) 

for infinite solution

D=D1=D2=D3=0

⇒5a=2b+c

x+y+(tanθ)z=0

question:
Let S be the set of all values of θ∈[-π,π] for which the system of linear equations
x+y+3z=0
-x+(tanθ)y+7z=0
x+y+(tanθ)z=0
has non-trivial solution.Then 120π∑0∈Sθ is equal to
solution:
Given,

S be the set of all values of θ∈[-π,π] for which the system of linear equations
x+y+3z=0
-x+(tanθ)y+7z=0
x+y+(tanθ)z=0
has non-trivial solution,

So, by condition of non-trivial solution we get,

113-1tanθ711tanθ=0

⇒tan2θ-7--tanθ-7+3-1-tanθ=0

⇒tan2θ+1-3tanθ-3=0

⇒tanθ+1tanθ-3=0

⇒tanθ=3,-1

⇒θ=π3,-2π3,-π4,3π4

Hence,

120π∑θ=12013-23-14+34=20

q:
The system of linear equations

3x-2y-kz=10

2x-4y-2z=6

x+2y-z=5 m
is inconsistent if :
question:
The system of linear equations

3x-2y-kz=10

2x-4y-2z=6

x+2y-z=5 m
is inconsistent if :
solution:
For inconsistent system of equation the conditions should be ∆=0 and at least one of the ∆x or ∆y or ∆z should not be equal to zero.

Now,

Δ=3-2-k2-4-212-1=0

⇒24+20-k8=0⇒k=3

And,

Δx=10-2-36-4-25 m2-1

⇒∆x=108-2-10 m+6-312+20 m

⇒∆x=84-5m

∆x≠0⇒m≠45

Δy=310-326-215 m-1

=3-6+10 m+100-310 m-6

=0

Δz=3-2102-46125m

⇒Δz=3-20m-12+210m-6+104+4

⇒Δz=-60m-36+20m-12+80

⇒Δz=-40m+32

⇒∆z=84-5m

∆z≠0⇒m≠45
